title: String Formatting & f-Strings
beginnerExplanation: Python offers multiple ways to embed variables inside strings: f-strings (f"Hello {name}"), .format(), and %-formatting. f-Strings (introduced in Python 3.6) are the modern, fastest, and most readable approach. They let you embed any Python expression directly inside curly braces.
intermediateExplanation: String concatenation with + gets messy fast: "Hello, " + name + "! You are " + str(age) + " years old." is unreadable. f-Strings solve this elegantly: f"Hello, {name}! You are {age} years old." — clean, fast, and powerful.
advancedExplanation:
f"Hello, {name}!"              # Variable
f"Total: {price:.2f}"           # 2 decimal places
f"Score: {score:>10}"           # Right-aligned, width 10
f"{items!r}"                    # repr() form
f"Result: {2 + 3}"              # Expression inside

realWorldAnalogy.title: Mad Libs Game
realWorldAnalogy.description: f-Strings are like Mad Libs — you write a template with blank slots {}, and Python fills them in with your variables. The template stays readable, and the blanks get dynamically filled at runtime.

mentalModel: f-Strings are like Mad Libs — you write a template with blank slots {}, and Python fills them in with your variables. The template stays readable, and the blanks get dynamically filled at runtime.

executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the f prefix — "Hello {name}" prints literally without the f.
Using f-strings in Python < 3.6 — they are not supported.
Trying to use backslashes inside f-string expressions — use a variable instead.

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use String Formatting & f-Strings but hit a common pitfall: Forgetting the f prefix — "Hello {name}" prints literally without the f.
debuggingMission.brokenCode:
f"Hello, {name}!"              # Variable
f"Total: {price:.2f}"           # 2 decimal places
f"Score: {score:>10}"           # Right-aligned, width 10
f"{items!r}"                    # repr() form
f"Result: {2 + 3}"              # Expression inside
debuggingMission.solution:
name = "Kael"
level = 7
hp = 85.5
print(f"⚔️ {name} | Level {level} | HP: {hp:.1f}")
print("=" * 30)
print(f"Next level in {100 - hp:.1f} HP")
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Use f-strings for all new code — they are the fastest and most readable option.

practiceQuestions.question: What does f"{price:.2f}" do when price = 9.5?
practiceQuestions.explanation: :.2f formats the number as a float with exactly 2 decimal places, so 9.5 becomes "9.50".

practiceQuestions.question: What happens if you write "Hello {name}" without the f prefix?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Without the f prefix, Python treats it as a regular string and {name} is printed literally, not replaced by the variable.

developerMode.industryUsage: String Formatting & f-Strings is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Use f-strings for all new code — they are the fastest and most readable option.
Use :.2f for currency/decimal formatting, :, for thousand separators.
Use :>10, :<10, :^10 for right, left, center alignment.
You can put any expression inside {} — even function calls and ternaries.
antiPatterns: Forgetting the f prefix — "Hello {name}" prints literally without the f.
Using f-strings in Python < 3.6 — they are not supported.
Trying to use backslashes inside f-string expressions — use a variable instead.
developerMode.architectureNotes: Interpreted and executed in the primary application call loop.
